1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. 11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying
44	   `IdentityComponent` to `G = PicScheme C`.
45	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **degree
46	   map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`, extracting the leading coefficient of the
47	   Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed
48	   degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
50	   — the **abelian-variety identification** of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: smooth, proper,
51	   geometrically irreducible `k`-group scheme of dimension `g(C)` --- the
52	   Jacobian variety of `C`.

54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.
61	- `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme G` — asserts the *existence* of an
62	  open immersion morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` whose underlying map of
63	  schemes is both an open and a closed immersion (clopen subscheme).
64	- `Pic0Scheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme.
65	- `PicScheme.degree C : (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ` —
66	  a genuine function from `k`-points to `ℤ`, not a constant.
67	- `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety C` — asserts the conjunction of the four
68	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
69	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
70	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/

112	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, the set of
113	connected components is finite.
114	
115	Proof: the map `irreducibleComponents α → ConnectedComponents α` sending each
116	irreducible component `C` to the connected component of an arbitrary chosen
117	point of `C` is well-defined (because `C` is preconnected, hence contained in
118	a single connected component) and surjective (because every point lies in
119	some irreducible component, namely `irreducibleComponent x`, which is a
120	subset of `connectedComponent x`). The source is finite by
121	`TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents`, so the
122	target is finite by `Finite.of_surjective`. -/
123	private lemma noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
124	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α] :
125	    Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := by
126	  classical
127	  have hfin : (irreducibleComponents α).Finite :=
128	    TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents
129	  haveI : Finite ↥(irreducibleComponents α) := hfin.to_subtype
130	  refine Finite.of_surjective
131	    (fun C : ↥(irreducibleComponents α) =>
132	      (ConnectedComponents.mk : α → ConnectedComponents α)
133	        ((C.property : Maximal IsIrreducible C.val).prop.nonempty.some)) ?_
134	  intro c
135	  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := ConnectedComponents.surjective_coe c
136	  refine ⟨⟨irreducibleComponent x, irreducibleComponent_mem_irreducibleComponents x⟩, ?_⟩
137	  rw [ConnectedComponents.coe_eq_coe]
138	  refine (connectedComponent_eq ?_).symm
139	  exact irreducibleComponent_subset_connectedComponent
140	    (isIrreducible_irreducibleComponent (x := x)).nonempty.some_mem
141	
142	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, each
143	connected component is open.
144	
145	Proof: `ConnectedComponents α` is totally disconnected
146	(`ConnectedComponents.totallyDisconnectedSpace`), hence T1
147	(`TotallyDisconnectedSpace.t1Space`), and finite by
148	`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`. A finite T1 space is discrete
149	(`Finite.instDiscreteTopology`), so the singleton
150	`{ConnectedComponents.mk x}` is open in the quotient. The preimage of this
151	singleton under the continuous quotient map `ConnectedComponents.mk` is
152	`connectedComponent x` (`connectedComponents_preimage_singleton`), which is
153	therefore open. -/
154	private lemma noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent
155	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α]
156	    (x : α) :
157	    IsOpen (connectedComponent x) := by
158	  haveI : Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
159	  haveI : DiscreteTopology (ConnectedComponents α) := inferInstance
160	  have h := (isOpen_discrete
161	    ({(ConnectedComponents.mk x : ConnectedComponents α)} : Set _)).preimage
162	    ConnectedComponents.continuous_coe
163	  rwa [connectedComponents_preimage_singleton] at h
164	
165	/-- Helper (iter-188): the **`LocallyConnectedSpace` instance** for the
166	underlying topological space of a `k`-scheme `G.left` whose structural
167	morphism `G.hom : G.left ⟶ Spec k` is locally of finite type.
168	
169	The substantive content is EGA I 6.1.9: a locally Noetherian topological
170	space has open connected components — equivalently, is locally connected.
171	Pushed through the chain: `Spec k` is Noetherian (a field has Noetherian
172	spectrum); `LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom + IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k) ⟹
173	IsLocallyNoetherian G.left` (Mathlib's
174	`AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`); and the
175	implication `IsLocallyNoetherian X ⟹ LocallyConnectedSpace X.toTopCat`
176	is the iter-188 project-side helper (`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`
177	+ `noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent`).
178	
179	The classical proof: each point `y ∈ G.left` has an open affine
180	neighbourhood `W = Spec R` with `R` Noetherian, hence `|W|` is a Noetherian
181	topological space; in a Noetherian space, each `connectedComponent y` is
182	clopen (finite irreducible components ⟹ finite connected components ⟹
183	finite T1 quotient ⟹ discrete quotient ⟹ singletons clopen in quotient ⟹
184	preimages clopen). Pulled back along the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left`,
185	this gives an open connected neighbourhood of `y` inside any open `F`
186	containing `y`. -/
187	private instance identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace
188	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
189	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
190	    [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
191	    LocallyConnectedSpace G.left := by
192	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian G.left := LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian G.hom
193	  rw [locallyConnectedSpace_iff_subsets_isOpen_isConnected]
194	  intro x F hF
195	  -- Extract an open neighbourhood `T ⊆ F` of `x`.
196	  obtain ⟨T, hTF, hT, hxT⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp hF
197	  -- Find an affine open `W ⊆ T` with `x ∈ W`.
198	  obtain ⟨W, hW, hxW, hWT⟩ :=
199	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := G.left) (U := ⟨T, hT⟩) hxT
200	  haveI : IsNoetherianRing Γ(G.left, W) :=
201	    IsLocallyNoetherian.component_noetherian ⟨W, hW⟩
202	  haveI hNoethW : TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace ↥W :=
203	    noetherianSpace_of_isAffineOpen W hW
204	  -- Inside `W` (Noetherian) the connected component of `x` is open; its image
205	  -- under the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left` is the connected open neighbourhood
206	  -- we want.
207	  let xW : ↥W := ⟨x, hxW⟩
208	  refine ⟨(Subtype.val : ↥W → G.left) '' connectedComponent xW, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
209	  · rintro _ ⟨z, _, rfl⟩
210	    exact hTF (hWT z.2)
211	  · exact W.isOpen.isOpenMap_subtype_val _
212	      (noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent xW)
213	  · exact ⟨xW, mem_connectedComponent, rfl⟩
214	  · exact isConnected_connectedComponent.image _ continuous_subtype_val.continuousOn
215	
216	/-- The image of the identity section `e : Spec k → G` (well-defined as a
217	single point of `|G|` because `Spec k` is a topological singleton). -/
218	private noncomputable def identitySectionPoint
219	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] : G.left :=
220	  ((MonObj.one (X := G)).left.base :
221	      ↥(Spec (.of k)) → G.left) (default : Spec (.of k))
222	
223	/-- Helper (iter-186; iter-187 closed): the **clopen carrier `Set`** of
224	the identity component of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type,
225	packaged as a `G.left.Opens`. The carrier set is `connectedComponent x`
226	for `x = e(*)` the image of the identity section
227	(`identitySectionPoint G`); openness is `isOpen_connectedComponent` which
228	needs the `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance above
229	(EGA I 6.1.9). -/
230	private noncomputable def identityComponentCarrier {k : Type u} [Field k]
231	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
232	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
233	    G.left.Opens :=
234	  ⟨connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G), isOpen_connectedComponent⟩

236	/-- The **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
237	finite type.
238	
239	Encoded as a `k`-scheme: an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))`, carrying the
240	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`
241	containing the identity section `e`, equipped with the open-subscheme
242	structure inherited from `G`". The associated open immersion
243	`IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` is the content of
244	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`.
245	
246	iter-186 body: built from the `identityComponentCarrier G` helper — the
247	open subscheme of `G` whose underlying topological space is the connected
248	component of `|G|` through the image of the identity section. The
249	structure morphism is the inherited `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`.
250	The substantive content (the actual carrier `Set`, plus its openness from
251	EGA I 6.1.9: locally Noetherian spaces have open connected components)
252	lives in the typed-sorry body of `identityComponentCarrier`. -/
253	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
254	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
255	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
256	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
257	  Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
258	
259	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
260	
261	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
262	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
263	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
264	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
265	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
266	subscheme).
267	
268	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
269	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
270	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
271	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
272	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
273	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
274	
275	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
276	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
277	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
278	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
279	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
280	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
281	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
282	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
283	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
284	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
285	identity section. -/
286	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
287	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
288	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
289	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
290	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
291	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
292	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
293	    simp [IdentityComponent]
294	  -- The over-morphism witness.
295	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
296	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
297	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
298	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
299	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
300	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
301	    infer_instance
302	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
303	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
304	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
305	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
306	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
307	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
308	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
309	    -- The carrier set is `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`, which is
310	    -- closed by `isClopen_connectedComponent.1` (with the
311	    -- `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance providing the closedness
312	    -- half of EGA I 6.1.9: connected components of a locally Noetherian topological
313	    -- space are clopen).
314	    change IsClosed (connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G))
315	    exact isClopen_connectedComponent.1

387	geometrically connected: for any field extension `K/k`, the pullback
388	`X ×_{Spec k} Spec K` is connected.

681	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}

213	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
214	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
215	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
216	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
217	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
218	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is

221	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By

232	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
233	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
235	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
236	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
237	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
238	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
239	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
240	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
241	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along

243	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)

282	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
283	% [\dots]
284	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
285	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
286	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
287	% [\dots]
288	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
289	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
290	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
291	\begin{proof}
292	  We establish
293	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
294	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
295	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
296	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
297	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
298	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
299	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
300	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
301	
302	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
303	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
304	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
305	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
306	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
307	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
308	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
309	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
310	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
311	
312	  \emph{Base-change commutation
313	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
314	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
315	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
316	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
317	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
318	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
319	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
320	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
321	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
322	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
323	
324	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
325	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
326	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
327	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
328	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
329	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
330	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
331	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
332	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
333	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
334	
335	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
337	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
338	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
339	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
340	  \(\overline k\), the product
341	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
342	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
343	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
344	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
345	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
346	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
347	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
348	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
349	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
350	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
351	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
352	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
353	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
354	\end{proof}

400	\section{The degree map}
401	\label{sec:pic_degree}
402	
403	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
404	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
405	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
406	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
407	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
408	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
409	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
410	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
411	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
412	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
413	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
414	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
415	degree.
416	
417	\begin{definition}\leanok
418	[Degree of a Picard class]
419	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
420	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
421	  
422	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
423	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
424	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
425	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
426	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
427	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
428	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
429	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
430	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
431	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
432	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
433	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
434	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
435	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
436	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
437	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
438	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
439	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
440	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
441	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
442	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
443	  \[
444	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
445	  \]
446	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
447	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
448	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
449	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
450	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
451	  \[
452	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
453	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
454	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
455	  \]
456	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
457	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
458	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
459	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
460	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
461	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
462	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
463	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
464	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
465	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
466	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
467	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
468	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
469	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
470	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
471	  of degree \(d\).
472	\end{definition}

474	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
475	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
476	
477	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
478	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
479	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
480	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
481	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
482	automatically.

571

581	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of

590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
592	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
593	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
594	% identity-component substrate.)
595	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
596	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
597	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
598	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
599	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
600	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
601	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
602	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
603	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
604	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
605	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
606	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
607	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
608	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
609	% everywhere."
610	\begin{proof}
611	  We establish
612	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
613	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
614	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
615	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
616	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
617	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
618	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
619	
620	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
621	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
622	  conjuncts from four inputs.
623	
624	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
625	  By
626	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
627	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
628	  type by
629	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
630	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
631	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
632	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
633	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
634	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
635	
636	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
637	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
638	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
639	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
640	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
641	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
642	
643	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
644	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
645	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
646	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
647	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
648	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
649	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
650	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
651	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
652	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
653	
654	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
655	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
656	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
657	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
658	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
659	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
660	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
661	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
662	
663	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
664	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
665	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
666	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
667	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
668	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
669	
670	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
671	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
672	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
673	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
674	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
675	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
676	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
677	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
678	  the open identity component, and
679	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
680	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
681	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
682	
683	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
684	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
685	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
686	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
687	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
688	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
689	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
690	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
691	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
692	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
693	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
694	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
695	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
696	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
697	  zero on \(C\)'').
698	\end{proof}

9	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Geometrically.Basic
10	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.UniversallyOpen
11	
12	/-!
13	# Geometrically connected schemes
14	
15	In this file we define geometrically connected morphisms of schemes. A morphism `f : X ⟶ Y` is
16	geometrically connected if for all `Spec K ⟶ Y` with `K` a field, `X ×[Y] Spec K` is connected.
17	In the case where `Y = Spec K` for some field `K` this recovers the standard definition
18	of a geometrically connected scheme over a field.
19	
20	## Main results
21	
22	- `AlgebraicGeometry.GeometricallyConnected`: A morphism `f : X ⟶ Y` is geometrically connected if
23	  for all `Spec K ⟶ Y` with `K` a field, `X ×[Y] Spec K` is connected.
24	- `GeometricallyConnected.iff_geometricallyConnected_fiber`: A scheme is geometrically connected
25	  over `S` iff the fibers of all `s : S` are geometrically connected.
26	
27	-/
28	
29	@[expose] public section
30	
31	open CategoryTheory MorphismProperty Limits
32	
33	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
34	
35	variable {X Y Z S : Scheme} (f : X ⟶ S) (g : Y ⟶ S)
36	
37	/-- We say that a morphism `f : X ⟶ Y` is geometrically connected if for all `Spec K ⟶ Y` with `K`
38	a field, `X ×[Y] Spec K` is connected. -/
39	@[mk_iff]
40	class GeometricallyConnected (f : X ⟶ Y) : Prop where
41	  geometrically_connectedSpace : geometrically (ConnectedSpace ·) f
42	
43	lemma GeometricallyConnected.eq_geometrically :
44	    @GeometricallyConnected = geometrically (ConnectedSpace ·) := by
45	  ext; exact geometricallyConnected_iff _
46	
47	instance : IsStableUnderBaseChange @GeometricallyConnected :=
48	  GeometricallyConnected.eq_geometrically ▸ inferInstance
49	
50	instance [GeometricallyConnected g] : GeometricallyConnected (pullback.fst f g) :=
51	  MorphismProperty.pullback_fst f g inferInstance
52	
53	instance [GeometricallyConnected f] : GeometricallyConnected (pullback.snd f g) :=
54	  MorphismProperty.pullback_snd f g inferInstance
55	
56	instance (V : S.Opens) [GeometricallyConnected f] : GeometricallyConnected (f ∣_ V) :=
57	  MorphismProperty.of_isPullback (isPullback_morphismRestrict ..).flip ‹_›
58	
59	instance (s : S) [GeometricallyConnected f] :
60	    GeometricallyConnected (f.fiberToSpecResidueField s) :=
61	  MorphismProperty.pullback_snd _ _ inferInstance
62	
63	instance (s : S) [GeometricallyConnected f] : ConnectedSpace (f.fiber s) :=
64	  GeometricallyConnected.geometrically_connectedSpace _ _ _ (.of_hasPullback _ _)
65	
66	instance (priority := low) [GeometricallyConnected f] : Surjective f :=
67	  ⟨fun x ↦ ⟨_, (f.range_fiberι x).le ⟨Nonempty.some inferInstance, rfl⟩⟩⟩
68	
69	lemma Scheme.Hom.isConnected_preimage_singleton [GeometricallyConnected f] (x : S) :
70	    _root_.IsConnected (f ⁻¹' {x}) := by
71	  rw [← f.range_fiberι, ← Set.image_univ]
72	  exact isConnected_univ.image _ (f.fiberι _).continuous.continuousOn
73	
74	lemma Scheme.Hom.isConnected_preimage [GeometricallyConnected f] (hf : IsOpenMap f)
75	    {s : Set S} (hs : _root_.IsConnected s) (hs' : IsClosed s) : _root_.IsConnected (f ⁻¹' s) := by
76	  refine Topology.IsCoinducing.isConnected_preimage_of_isClosed f.isConnected_preimage_singleton
77	    ?_ hs' hs
78	  exact (hf.isQuotientMap f.continuous f.surjective).isCoinducing
79	
80	/-- If `f : X ⟶ S` is geometrically connected and open,
81	then `f` induces a homeomorphism between the connected components of `X` and `S`. -/
82	@[simps! apply]
83	noncomputable
84	def Scheme.Hom.connectedComponentsHomeomorph [GeometricallyConnected f] (hf : IsOpenMap f) :
85	    ConnectedComponents X ≃ₜ ConnectedComponents S :=
86	  (hf.isQuotientMap f.continuous f.surjective).isCoinducing.connectedComponentsHomeomorph
87	    f.isConnected_preimage_singleton
88	
89	lemma GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace [GeometricallyConnected f] [ConnectedSpace S]
90	    (hf : IsOpenMap f) :
91	    ConnectedSpace X := by
92	  simpa [connectedSpace_iff_univ] using f.isConnected_preimage hf isConnected_univ
93	
94	/-- If `X` is geometrically connected over a point, then it is connected. -/
95	lemma GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace_of_subsingleton
96	    [GeometricallyConnected f] [Subsingleton S] [Nonempty S] : ConnectedSpace X :=
97	  have : ConnectedSpace S := ⟨‹_›⟩
98	  GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace (f := f) fun _ _ ↦ isOpen_discrete _
99	
100	instance [GeometricallyConnected f] [UniversallyOpen f] [ConnectedSpace Y] :
101	    ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback f g) :=
102	  GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace (pullback.snd _ _) (pullback.snd f g).isOpenMap
103	
104	instance [GeometricallyConnected g] [UniversallyOpen g] [ConnectedSpace X] :
105	    ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback f g) :=
106	  GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace (pullback.fst _ _) (pullback.fst f g).isOpenMap
107	
108	lemma GeometricallyConnected.iff_geometricallyConnected_fiber :
109	    GeometricallyConnected f ↔ ∀ s, GeometricallyConnected (f.fiberToSpecResidueField s) := by
110	  simp only [eq_geometrically, ← geometrically_iff_forall_fiberToSpecResidueField]
111	
112	lemma GeometricallyConnected.comp
113	    (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) [GeometricallyConnected f] [GeometricallyConnected g]
114	    [UniversallyOpen f] [UniversallyOpen g] :
115	    GeometricallyConnected (f ≫ g) := by
116	  refine ⟨geometrically_iff_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms.mpr fun K _ x ↦ ?_⟩
117	  rw [← (pullbackRightPullbackFstIso g x f).hom.homeomorph.connectedSpace_iff]
118	  infer_instance
119	
120	end AlgebraicGeometry

12	/-!
13	# Geometrically-`P` schemes over a field
14	
15	In this file we define the basic interface for properties like geometrically reduced,
16	geometrically irreducible, geometrically connected etc. In this file
17	we treat an abstract property of schemes `P` and derive the general properties that are
18	shared by all of these variants.
19	
20	A morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ Y` is geometrically `P` if for any field `K` and
21	morphism `Spec K ⟶ Y`, the base change `X ×[Y] Spec K` satisfies `P`.
22	
23	## Main definitions and results
24	
25	- `AlgebraicGeometry.geometrically`: The morphism property of geometrically-`P` morphisms
26	- `AlgebraicGeometry.geometrically_iff_forall_fiberToSpecResidueField`: `f : X ⟶ Y` is
27	  geometrically-`P` if and only if for every `y : Y`, the fiber `f ⁻¹ {y}` is geometrically-`P`
28	  over `Spec κ(y)`.
29	
30	## Notes
31	
32	This contribution was created as part of the Formalising Algebraic Geometry workshop 2025 in
33	Heidelberg.
34	-/
35	
36	@[expose] public section
37	
38	universe u
39	
40	open CategoryTheory Limits CommRingCat
41	
42	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
43	
44	/-- A morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ Y` is geometrically `P` if for any field `K` and
45	morphism `Spec K ⟶ Y`, the base change `X ×[Y] Spec K` satisfies `P`. -/
46	def geometrically (P : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u}) : MorphismProperty Scheme.{u} :=
47	  fun X Y f ↦ ∀ ⦃K : Type u⦄ [Field K] (y : Spec (.of K) ⟶ Y)
48	    ⦃Z : Scheme.{u}⦄ (fst : Z ⟶ X) (snd : Z ⟶ Spec (.of K)),
49	    IsPullback fst snd f y → P Z
50	
51	lemma geometrically_eq_universally (P : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u}) :
52	    geometrically P = .universally fun X Y _ ↦ IsIntegral Y → Subsingleton Y → P X := by
53	  ext X Y f
54	  refine ⟨fun hf Z W snd q fst h _ _ ↦ ?_, fun hf aK y Z W fst snd h ↦ ?_⟩
55	  · let := (isField_of_isIntegral_of_subsingleton W).toField
56	    apply hf (W.isoSpec.inv ≫ q) snd (fst ≫ W.isoSpec.hom)
57	    apply h.flip.of_iso (.refl _) (.refl _) W.isoSpec (.refl _) <;> simp
58	  · exact hf _ _ _ h.flip inferInstance inferInstance
59	
60	lemma geometrically_inf (P Q : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u}) :
61	    geometrically (P ⊓ Q) = geometrically P ⊓ geometrically Q := by
62	  simp only [geometrically_eq_universally, ← MorphismProperty.universally_inf]
63	  congr with X Y f
64	  exact ⟨fun H ↦ ⟨(H · · |>.1), (H · · |>.2)⟩, fun H a b ↦ ⟨H.1 a b, H.2 a b⟩⟩
65	
66	variable (P : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u})
67	
68	instance : (geometrically P).IsStableUnderBaseChange := by
69	  rw [geometrically_eq_universally]
70	  infer_instance
71	
72	instance [P.IsClosedUnderIsomorphisms] : IsZariskiLocalAtTarget (geometrically P) := by
73	  rw [geometrically_eq_universally]
74	  refine universally_isZariskiLocalAtTarget _ fun {X} Y f ι U hU H _ _ ↦ ?_
75	  obtain ⟨y⟩ := (inferInstance : Nonempty Y)
76	  obtain ⟨i, hy⟩ := hU.exists_mem y
77	  have heq : U i = ⊤ := eq_top_iff.mpr fun z _ ↦ by rwa [Subsingleton.elim z y]
78	  let e : ↑(U i) ≅ Y := Y.isoOfEq heq ≪≫ Y.topIso
79	  let e' : ↑(f ⁻¹ᵁ U i) ≅ X := X.isoOfEq (by simp [heq]) ≪≫ X.topIso
80	  exact P.prop_of_iso e' <| H i (.of_isIso e.inv) (e.hom.homeomorph.subsingleton_congr.mpr ‹_›)
81	
82	section geometrically
83	
84	variable {P : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u}} {X Y : Scheme.{u}} {f : X ⟶ Y}
85	
86	lemma pullback_of_geometrically (hf : geometrically P f) (K : Type u) [Field K]
87	    (y : Spec (.of K) ⟶ Y) : P (Limits.pullback f y) :=
88	  hf _ _ _ (.of_hasPullback _ _)
89	
90	lemma pullback_of_geometrically' (hf : geometrically P f) (K : Type u) [Field K]
91	    (y : Spec (.of K) ⟶ Y) : P (Limits.pullback y f) :=
92	  hf _ _ _ (.flip <| .of_hasPullback _ _)
93	
94	lemma geometrically_iff_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms [P.IsClosedUnderIsomorphisms] :
95	    geometrically P f ↔
96	      ∀ (K : Type u) [Field K] (y : Spec (.of K) ⟶ Y), P (Limits.pullback f y) := by
97	  refine ⟨fun h K _ _ ↦ pullback_of_geometrically h _ _, fun H K _ _ Y fst snd h ↦ ?_⟩
98	  exact P.prop_of_iso h.isoPullback.symm (H _ _)
99	
100	lemma fiber_of_geometrically (hf : geometrically P f) (y : Y) : P (f.fiber y) :=
101	  pullback_of_geometrically hf _ _
102	
103	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
104	/-- `P` holds geometrically for `f` if and only if all fibers are geometrically `P`. -/
105	lemma geometrically_iff_forall_fiberToSpecResidueField :
106	    geometrically P f ↔ ∀ (y : Y), geometrically P (f.fiberToSpecResidueField y) := by
107	  refine ⟨fun hf y ↦ (geometrically P).pullback_snd _ _ hf, fun H ↦ ?_⟩
108	  intro K _ y Z fst snd h
109	  obtain ⟨⟨y, φ⟩, rfl⟩ := (Scheme.SpecToEquivOfField _ _).symm.surjective y
110	  let p : Z ⟶ f.fiber y :=
111	    pullback.lift fst (snd ≫ Spec.map φ) (by simp [h.w, Scheme.SpecToEquivOfField])
112	  apply H y (Spec.map φ) p snd
113	  simp only [Scheme.SpecToEquivOfField, Equiv.coe_fn_symm_mk] at h
114	  refine .flip (.of_bot (.flip ?_) ?_ (IsPullback.of_hasPullback f (Y.fromSpecResidueField y)).flip)
115	  · convert h
116	    simp [p]
117	  · simp [p, Scheme.Hom.fiberToSpecResidueField]
118	
119	/-- This holds in particular if `Y = Spec K`. -/
120	lemma self_of_isIntegral_of_geometrically [IsIntegral Y] [Subsingleton Y] (hf : geometrically P f) :
121	    P X := by
122	  rw [geometrically_eq_universally] at hf
123	  exact MorphismProperty.universally_le _ _ hf ‹_› ‹_›
124	
125	variable {P : ObjectProperty Scheme.{u}} {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {f : X ⟶ Spec (.of R)}
126	
127	lemma geometrically_iff_of_commRing :
128	    geometrically P f ↔ ∀ ⦃K : Type u⦄ [Field K] [Algebra R K] ⦃Y : Scheme.{u}⦄ (fst : Y ⟶ X)
129	      (snd : Y ⟶ Spec (.of K)), IsPullback fst snd f (Spec.map <| ofHom (algebraMap R K)) →
130	      P Y := by
131	  refine ⟨fun hs K _ _ Z fst snd h ↦ hs _ _ _ h, fun H K _ y Z fst snd h ↦ ?_⟩
132	  obtain ⟨φ, rfl⟩ := Spec.map_surjective y
133	  algebraize [φ.hom]
134	  exact H fst snd h
135	
136	lemma geometrically_iff_of_commRing_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms [P.IsClosedUnderIsomorphisms] :
137	    geometrically P f ↔ ∀ (K : Type u) [Field K] [Algebra R K],
138	      P (Limits.pullback f (Spec.map <| ofHom <| algebraMap R K)) := by
139	  refine ⟨fun hf K _ _ ↦ pullback_of_geometrically hf _ _, fun H ↦ ?_⟩
140	  rw [geometrically_iff_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms]
141	  intro K _ y
142	  obtain ⟨φ, rfl⟩ := Spec.map_surjective y
143	  algebraize [φ.hom]
144	  exact H K
145	
146	end geometrically
147	
148	end AlgebraicGeometry

482	`geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` helper): the structural

486	- `identityComponent_connectedSpace`: `ConnectedSpace (IdentityComponent G).left`

488	- `identityComponentSection_isSection`: existence of a section

500	private instance identityComponent_geometricallyConnected
501	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
502	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
503	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
504	    GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent G).hom :=
505	  geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section
506	    (IdentityComponent G).hom (identityComponentSection G)

509	/-- **The identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism.**
510	
511	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (b): the clopen subscheme `G^0`
512	inherits a `k`-group-scheme structure from `G`, and the inclusion morphism
513	`G^0 ↪ G` (from `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`) is compatible
514	with the group laws on source and target. The statement-level pin asserts
515	the existence of the inherited `GrpObj` structure; the compatibility of
516	the inclusion with the group operations is the substantive content of
517	Kleiman's argument (the product `G^0 ×_k G^0` is connected by
518	EGA IV₂ 4.5.8; the group-multiplication map sends this connected subset
519	containing the identity into the connected component `G^0`).
520	
521	The full statement (existence of `GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)` *together*
522	with the homomorphism-compatibility of the inclusion in the relevant
523	`Hom`-form) lives in iter-193+. -/
524	theorem IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism {k : Type u} [Field k]
525	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
526	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
527	    Nonempty (GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)) :=
528	  sorry
529	
530	/-- **The identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible.**
531	
532	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (c): the open subgroup `G^0` of
533	a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type is itself
534	locally-of-finite-type-plus-quasi-compact (i.e., of finite type) over `k`,
535	and is geometrically irreducible. The proof reduces (after base change to
536	`\bar k`) to picking a nonempty smooth affine open subset `U ⊆ G^0`; the
537	translates `hg⁻¹U` give an open cover of `G^0` by smooth, hence irreducible,
538	neighbourhoods, so `G^0` is locally irreducible at every closed point; with
539	connectedness this gives irreducibility globally (EGA I 6.1.10). The image
540	`α(U × U) = G^0` is the image of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme
541	`U × U`, so `G^0` is quasi-compact.
542	
543	iter-189 partial progress: the `LocallyOfFiniteType` conjunct closes
544	axiom-clean via composition of an open immersion with `G.hom`. The
545	remaining two conjuncts (`QuasiCompact`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`)

548	theorem IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible
549	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
550	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
551	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
552	    LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
553	      QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
554	      GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
555	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
556	  · -- `LocallyOfFiniteType`: `(IdentityComponent G).hom` unfolds to
557	    -- `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`. Open immersion ∘ LFT
558	    -- is LFT (`locallyOfFiniteType_of_isOpenImmersion` +
559	    -- `locallyOfFiniteType_comp`).
560	    change LocallyOfFiniteType ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
561	    infer_instance
562	  · -- `QuasiCompact ∧ GeometricallyIrreducible`: Kleiman's group-theoretic
563	    -- argument. `α(U × U) = G^0` quasi-compact via the affine `U × U`;
564	    -- geometric irreducibility via base change to `\bar k`, picking a
565	    -- smooth affine `U ⊆ G^0`, and translating `hg⁻¹U` to cover. Cannot
566	    -- close axiom-clean without the substrate `isSubgroupHomomorphism`
567	    -- + EGA IV₂ 4.5.8 / 4.6.1 / EGA I 6.1.10 (not yet in Mathlib).
568	    sorry
569	
570	/-- **Formation of the identity component commutes with base change.**
571	
572	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (d): for any field extension
573	`K/k`, the natural comparison map `(G^0)_K → (G_K)^0` is an isomorphism of
574	`K`-schemes. The proof: `G^0` is geometrically connected as a connected
575	scheme with a `k`-rational point (EGA IV₂ 4.5.14), so its base change
576	`(G^0)_K` remains connected; it is also open and closed in `G_K`, so
577	coincides with the identity component `(G_K)^0`.
578	
579	The statement-level pin asserts existence of: a `K`-group-scheme structure
580	on the base change `G ×_{Spec k} Spec K` (with appropriate
581	locally-of-finite-type instance), and an isomorphism on underlying schemes
582	identifying the two iterated constructions.
583	
584	iter-192 partial closure (Lane A.3.i, axiom-clean modulo the iso slot):
585	the `_grpInst` slot closes via `CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd`
586	(per `analogies/lane-a3i-isconnected-prod.md` second analogue — the
587	category-theoretic base-change of `GrpObj` directly fitting the
588	`Over.mk (pullback.snd ...)` shape of `G_K`); the `_locFTInst` slot
589	closes via Mathlib's stability of `LocallyOfFiniteType` under base
590	change (`inferInstance` fires on the pullback). The remaining iso slot
591	`(IdentityComponent G_K).left ≅ pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ`
592	is the substantive content of Stacks 04KS / EGA IV₂ 4.5.16: identifying
593	the iterated identity-component construction with the base change of
594	the identity component, which requires the
595	`geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` helper above plus
596	descent of clopen partitions along base change (not yet in Mathlib). -/
597	theorem IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
598	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
599	    (K : Type u) [Field K] [Algebra k K] :
600	    let φ : Spec (CommRingCat.of K) ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of k) :=
601	      Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k K))
602	    let G_K : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of K)) :=
603	      Over.mk (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.snd G.hom φ)
604	    Nonempty (Σ' (_grpInst : GrpObj G_K)
605	                 (_locFTInst : LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom),
606	      (IdentityComponent G_K).left ≅
607	        CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ) := by
608	  intro φ G_K
609	  -- `GrpObj G_K` via the category-theoretic base-change lemma
610	  -- `CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd`. The instance bridge
611	  -- `[GrpObj G] ⟹ [GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom)]` is by defeq (`G = Over.mk G.hom`
612	  -- when `G` is an over-category object whose right component is the unique
613	  -- inhabitant of `Discrete PUnit`).
614	  haveI hG : GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom) := ‹GrpObj G›
615	  haveI hGK_grp : GrpObj G_K := CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd
616	  -- `LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom`: `G_K.hom = pullback.snd G.hom φ` is the
617	  -- base change of `G.hom` along `φ`; `LocallyOfFiniteType` is stable
618	  -- under base change (Mathlib instance).
619	  haveI hGK_lft : LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom :=
620	    (inferInstance : LocallyOfFiniteType (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.snd G.hom φ))
621	  -- The iso slot: substrate is now in place (iter-193 push-beyond):
622	  -- `identityComponent_geometricallyConnected` provides
623	  -- `GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent G).hom`, which combined with
624	  -- Mathlib's `[GeometricallyConnected f] [UniversallyOpen f]
625	  -- [ConnectedSpace Y] ⟹ ConnectedSpace (pullback f g)` instance gives
626	  -- `ConnectedSpace (pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ)` (the connected-
627	  -- pullback substrate). The iso construction proceeds by:
